title: Think it through
description: Question claims, weigh evidence, and don't get fooled.
summaryPoints: Separate facts you can verify from opinions and unsupported claims
Size up a source: who said it, what's their evidence, who benefits
Spot fallacies like bandwagon, ad hominem, and false cause
Read laterally — leave the page and see what others say about it
Catch your own confirmation bias before it does the thinking for you

content.title: Don't swallow it whole
content.body:
A post shows up in the group chat: "Scientists say studying with music makes you 40% smarter." Three people already shared it.

Critical thinking is the pause before you hit share. It's not being negative — it's refusing to swallow a claim whole just because it sounds good or everyone's repeating it.

You ask: is that actually true? Who said it? Where's the proof?
content.imageAlt: A magnifying glass examining a lightbulb

content.title: Fact, opinion, or just a claim
content.body:
Three different things often get jumbled together:

- **Fact** — checkable against reality. "Water boils at 100°C at sea level."
- **Opinion** — someone's preference or judgment. "Rainy days are the best for studying."
- **Unsupported claim** — sounds like a fact but has no evidence behind it. "This app rewires your brain in a week."

The trap is treating the third one like the first.

items.text: The exam is on Tuesday at 9am
History is way more interesting than chemistry
This is the hardest class in the whole school

content.prompt: An unsupported claim counts as a fact as long as it sounds confident.
content.explanation: Confidence isn't evidence. A claim only becomes a fact when you can actually verify it.

content.title: Check the source
content.body:
Before you trust a claim, size up where it came from:

- **Who's saying it?** A doctor, a random account, an ad?
- **What's their evidence?** Data and studies, or just vibes?
- **Do they benefit?** Someone selling the thing isn't a neutral judge.
- **Are they biased?** Everyone leans somewhere — notice it.
- **Is it current?** A 2009 stat about phones is ancient history.

No evidence, big payday for them? Be extra suspicious.

content.prompt: Which source is most credible for whether a study trick works?
content.context: You want to know if rewriting notes by hand actually helps you remember.
options: An influencer's ad for a $40 note-taking course
A random comment that says 'trust me, it works'
A peer-reviewed study comparing handwriting and typing
A meme you saw three times this week
content.explanation: A study with real evidence beats sources that are selling something, anonymous, or just repeated a lot.

content.prompt: Match each thinking trap to what it actually is.

pairs.right: Everyone believes it, so it's true
Attack the person, not the point

content.title: Fallacies to dodge
content.body:
Fallacies are sneaky shortcuts that feel convincing but prove nothing:

- **Bandwagon** — "everyone's doing it" so it must be right.
- **Ad hominem** — trash the person instead of their argument.
- **False cause** — two things happen together, so one caused the other.
- **False dilemma** — pretending there are only two choices.
- **Appeal to emotion** — scaring or hyping you past the logic.
- **Cherry-picking** — showing only the data that fits.

content.prompt: If ice cream sales and sunburns rise together, ice cream must cause sunburns.
content.explanation: Correlation isn't causation — hot weather drives both. That's the false-cause trap.

content.prompt: Which line is the bandwagon fallacy?
options: This app has three studies showing it improves recall
Everyone in class uses this app, so it must be the best
I tried this app and my grades went up over a month
The app is free, which makes it worth testing
content.explanation: Popularity isn't proof. 'Everyone does it' is the bandwagon move, no matter how many people pile on.

content.instructions: One word makes this advice backwards. Tap it.
content.sentence: Confirmation bias makes you favor information that disagrees with what you already believe.
content.explanation: Confirmation bias pulls you toward info that agrees with you — which is exactly why you have to check the other side on purpose.

content.title: Read sideways, not down
content.body:
When a sketchy site says "we're a trusted health authority," don't just scroll down their own page — of course they'll praise themselves.

**Lateral reading** means leaving the page and opening new tabs to see what other, independent sources say about that source. Fact-checkers do this first.

A single page can lie all day. A web of independent sources is much harder to fake.

content.prompt: Put the steps for evaluating a claim in order.
steps: Notice the claim and what it's really saying
Check who's saying it and what evidence they have
Read laterally to see what other sources say
Look for the other side and another explanation
Decide whether to trust, doubt, or share it
content.explanation: Spot it, source it, cross-check it, weigh the other side, then decide — judging before you investigate is how you get fooled.

content.prompt: Leaving a page to see what other sources say about it is called ___ reading.
content.explanation: Lateral reading checks a source from the outside instead of trusting what it says about itself.

content.prompt: A shocking headline makes you furious before you finish reading. What's the smart first move?
options: Pause and ask what evidence backs the claim
Share it fast while you're still fired up
Trust it because it made you feel something strong
Assume it's true since it confirms what you suspected
content.explanation: Strong emotion is a flag, not proof — appeal to emotion works by rushing you past the evidence.

content.title: Is it true?
content.intro: A jaw-dropping post is going viral: "BREAKING: 8 hours of sleep secretly LOWERS your test scores!" Your finger's on share. Keep the Truth-o-meter high.

steps.situation: The post has no author and links to nothing. What first?

options.text: Check who posted it and what evidence they show
options.feedback: Source first — no name and no evidence is a giant red flag.
Shock value isn't truth. You just spread an unchecked claim.

options.text: Believe it — a friend reposted it
options.feedback: A friend sharing it doesn't make it true. That's bandwagon thinking.

steps.situation: You find the original 'source': an ad for a $30 energy-drink that 'replaces sleep.'

options.feedback: A seller grading their own product isn't neutral evidence.

options.text: Read laterally — see what real sleep researchers say
options.feedback: Independent sources beat a page that's selling you something.

options.text: Accept it — it fits your hope that sleep is overrated
options.feedback: That's confirmation bias deciding for you.

steps.situation: Every independent source says sleep HELPS memory and scores. The post cherry-picked one odd graph.

options.text: Trust the weight of independent evidence, don't share the post
options.feedback: You weighed the other side and followed the evidence. That's the whole skill.

options.text: Share anyway — the one weird graph is interesting
options.feedback: Cherry-picked data isn't the full picture. Don't amplify it.

content.outro: You questioned it, sourced it, read laterally, and let evidence — not the headline — decide. That's critical thinking.
